nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reefs and rocky inshore areas; commonly associated with Aiptasia anemones on hard substrate

diet.notes: The Berghia Nudibranch is an obligate predator of Aiptasia anemones (including Aiptasia pallida and related species), meaning it will not accept any other food. Once the Aiptasia population in the aquarium is exhausted — typically within a few weeks — the animals will starve unless transferred to another tank with a fresh infestation. Berghia locate their prey chemically through their rhinophores, the pair of sensory appendages on the head.

summary: Small captive-bred aeolid sea slug sold as a biological control for pest Aiptasia anemones; obligate Aiptasia predator that is otherwise reef-safe.
careNotes: The Berghia Nudibranch (Berghia stephanieae) is purchased specifically as a biological pest-control agent against Aiptasia (glass anemones) in reef tanks. It is an OBLIGATE Aiptasia predator and will not accept any substitute food — once the Aiptasia population in the tank is depleted, the colony will starve unless the keeper transfers individuals to another infested system. At least 3–6 individuals are generally advised for any tank (and roughly one per 40 L / 10 gal beyond that), because Berghia are nocturnal, cryptic, and need conspecifics to locate mates and hunt effectively. Recommended reef-tank parameters run roughly 22–26 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, and a specific gravity of 1.023–1.025. Pump intakes and powerheads should be screened — Berghia are soft-bodied and easily shredded. They are highly sensitive to copper-based medications and to any rapid swing in salinity or temperature; drip-acclimation over 30–60 minutes is strongly recommended. The species is sometimes still sold under the older misapplied name 'Berghia verrucicornis' (a separate Mediterranean species) and under the synonym Aeolidiella stephanieae; the currently accepted name is Berghia stephanieae. Many otherwise reef-safe tankmates will opportunistically eat Berghia — notably peppermint shrimp, coral banded shrimp, arrow crabs, wrasses, hawkfish, dottybacks, and filefish. Adding Berghia after lights-out, when most predators are quiescent, tends to improve survival.
breedingNotes: Hermaphroditic but requires a second individual to fertilize; this is why stocking densities below ~3 are discouraged. Sexually mature at about 1 cm body length. Mated individuals lay spiral egg coils every few days in hidden crevices on live rock or aquarium glass; coils range from ~50 eggs (young adults) to over 1,000 (large adults). Incubation runs about two weeks, after which veliger-stage larvae emerge and metamorphose directly without a planktonic phase, making this one of the rare nudibranchs successfully captive-bred at commercial scale.

primary.name: AlgaeBarn
primary.notes: Designated primary LiveAquaria product page returned 404 — swapped to AlgaeBarn (a major captive-bred Berghia retailer) as the working primary. Confirmed water parameters (72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C, pH 8.2–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12), reef-safety, Aiptasia-obligate diet, and adult size (~1 inch / 2.5 cm).

additional.name: SaltwaterAquariumBlog – Berghia care guide
additional.notes: Designated secondary Reef2Reef Wiki returned 403 — swapped to this dedicated Berghia care guide. Cross-confirmed scientific name (Berghia stephanieae, older B. verrucicornis), lifespan (~10–12 months), obligate Aiptasia diet, hermaphroditic reproduction (egg coils every few days), peaceful temperament, and predator list (peppermint shrimp, wrasses, hawkfish, arrow crabs, filefish).

additional.name: WoRMS – Berghia stephanieae
additional.notes: Taxonomic authority for current accepted name (Berghia stephanieae Valdés, 2005), family Aeolidiidae, order Nudibranchia, and synonymy with Aeolidiella stephanieae.
